annotations.text: This patient's acute presentation with fever, chills, an elevated white-cell count of 15,500, and a sedimentation rate of 81 mm per hour certainly suggests an infectious cause.
The symptoms of a hydatid cyst are caused principally by the pressure of the expanding cyst, which is estimated to grow between 1 and 5 cm a year.
The major problem with this diagnosis is the negative enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for anti-amebic antibodies on the fourth hospital day.

Therefore, sequential serologic testing and surgical intervention to obtain material for microscopical examination, Gram stain, and bacteriologic culture are warranted in patients with a hepatic abscess and nonreactive serologic tests for antibodies to  *Echin. histolytic
I think that the most likely diagnosis in this case is a primary amebic liver abscess. Although I am troubled by the negative serologic test, there are certainly precedents for its occurrence.
This finding may have reflected inability to respond antigenically, although we have no other evidence of an immune deficiency in this boy.
The finding of a large, solitary mass within the right lobe of the liver is typical of an amebic abscess and is present in the great majority of the cases.

If the serologic test for amebae had been positive, further medical treatment would have been appropriate, since 72 hours are often required to observe a clinical response after the initiation of anti-amebic therapy.
If the mass appears to be hydatid disease at operation, a scolecidal agent should be applied to the abscess.
Alternatively, if an amebic or bacterial abscess is identified at operation, simple debridement and drainage should be performed.

nd we were initially concerned about an infection. However, when we reviewed the diagnostic images we believed that the ultrasound finding of a predominantly solid mass with cystic components indicated a tumor, either a benign mesenchymal hamartoma or a malignant mesenchymoma
The Surgical Service was consulted early during this child's admission, and we were initially concerned about an infection.
The gross and histologic features of the case under discussion are typical, with round, spindle-shaped, and stellate cells in a myxoid background with admixed larger cells.
